segments.purgatorio-20-g16: Noi is the explicit subject pronoun “we.” Andavam is the imperfect first‑person plural (archaic spelling for modern andavamo) of andare, giving a continuous/past narrative sense “we were going/we went.” The phrase con passi lenti e scarsi is a circumstantial prepositional phrase (with + noun + coordinated adjectives) describing the manner of walking; scarsi is an adjective meaning ‘sparse/few/short’ modifying passi.
segments.purgatorio-20-g17: e is the coordinating conjunction “and.” io is the emphatic subject pronoun “I,” placed here for emphasis. attento is a predicate adjective meaning “attentive,” used elliptically (the copula ‘was’ is understood but omitted). a l’ombre is the prepositional phrase ‘to the shadows’; a + l’ is an older contraction corresponding to modern alle (a + le) before the feminine plural ombre.
segments.purgatorio-20-g18: ch’ (= che) introduces a subordinate clause “that/which.” i’ is the clitic form of io “I.” sentia is the imperfect of sentire; here it functions as a verb of perception best translated “I heard.” pietosamente is an adverb (archaic spelling) modifying how the following actions were done: piangere e lagnarsi are infinitives governed by the perception verb sentia — literal structure: “I heard (them) to weep and to lament piteously,” so the infinitives express what was heard. lagnarsi is a reflexive infinitive meaning “lament (themselves)/complain.”
segments.purgatorio-20-g19: e = “and.” per ventura is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning “by chance/perhaps”; per here has the sense of “by”/“by means of” within that idiom. udi’ is an archaic/synoptic spelling (the apostrophe marks omitted vowel) of udii/udì, first‑person singular aorist/simple past of udire, meaning “I heard.” The quoted exclamation «Dolce Maria!» is direct speech; Dolce is an epithet (‘sweet’ or more naturally ‘dear’) addressing Mary.
segments.purgatorio-20-g20: This phrase is an impersonal or passive-seeming report of action. dinanzi a noi = 'in front of us/before us' (dinanzi + a + noi); chiamar is the bare infinitive used in narrative compression (common in older/poetic Italian) to report what they did — 'to call/cry out'; così modifies chiamar; nel pianto is a prepositional phrase ('in tears') and the final clause come fa donna che in parturir sia is a simile introduced by come + fa + noun + relative clause with subjunctive sia ('who is/should be in childbirth').
segments.purgatorio-20-g21: e seguitar: is a brief connective ('and they continued / and following that:'). seguitar is a literary infinitive used in place of a finite verb to report the next action and introduces the following quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-20-g22: This begins the quoted speech. Povera is a direct address ('Poor one' / 'you poor thing'); fosti is the archaic or dialectal past form expected to mean 'you were' (but the supplied senses list does not include the correct verbal sense); tanto intensifies the adjective ('so / so very').
segments.purgatorio-20-g23: The remainder of the quotation gives the measure of that poverty. quanto ... si può = 'as much as can be' (quanto ... si può + verb); veder si può uses the impersonal si + può ('one can see' / 'it can be seen'); per quello ospizio = 'by/from that lodging/shelter' (per here indicating source or evidence); dove sponesti il tuo portato santo is a relative clause ('where you pledged/offered your holy burden') with sponesti = 2nd person past (you pledged/betrothed/offered).
segments.purgatorio-20-g24: Seguentemente intesi is a simple narrative clause: seguentemente = 'subsequently/then' and intesi is the past of intendere used here in the sense 'I understood / I heard' (older Italian often uses intendere for perceiving/hearing). It introduces reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-20-g25: A vocative address inside the quotation. O is the exclamatory particle 'O/oh'; buon is an adjective used before the name as a term of approbation; Fabrizio is a proper name in the vocative.
segments.purgatorio-20-g26: This is a compact comparative preference. con povertà volesti anzi virtute = 'you wished/chose poverty, rather (anzi) virtue' — volesti is 2nd person past of volere ('you wished/chose'). che in this construction functions as the comparative 'than' linking to the following clause (gran ricchezza posseder con vizio = 'to possess great wealth with/by vice'), so the sentence means you preferred poverty/virtue to possessing wealth by vice.
segments.purgatorio-20-g27: Queste parole m’eran sì piaciute = 'These words had so pleased me.' m’eran is the contracted imperfect/pluperfect auxiliary structure (mi + eran), with piaciute as the past participle; sì intensifies the verb. The construction is typical of older/poetic Italian.
segments.purgatorio-20-g28: ch’io mi trassi oltre per aver contezza di quello spirto onde parean venute = a result clause introduced by ch’ (che = 'that/so that'). mi trassi (first-person past) is reflexive 'I drew myself/stepped forward'; per + infinitive (per aver) indicates purpose 'in order to have/obtain' contezza = 'knowledge/awareness'; di quello spirto = 'of that spirit'; onde parean venute uses onde = 'from/whence' + parean (imperfect plural of parere) 'they seemed' + venute 'to have come', so 'from which they seemed to have come.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g1: 'Contra' is the preposition 'against'. 'miglior voler' is a poetic noun phrase where 'miglior' (comparative 'better') modifies 'voler' (the noun 'will' or 'wish'); the second 'voler' functions as the subject 'a bad will'. 'mal' qualifies that second 'voler' ('bad' or 'evil'), and 'pugna' here functions as a verb (3rd person singular present of pugnare) 'fights' — a compact, inverted poetic order: 'Against a better will, an evil will fights.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g2: 'onde' is a classical/poetic connective meaning 'wherefore'/'therefore' (not the literal 'wave'); 'contra' again means 'against'; '’l' is the clitic article 'the'; 'piacer' is the noun 'pleasure' or 'my liking'; 'mio' is the possessive 'my'. The phrase means 'therefore, against my pleasure'.
segments.purgatorio-20-g3: 'per' here introduces purpose: 'in order to'. 'piacerli' is the short infinitival cluster from 'piacere + clitic' meaning 'to please him/them' (literally 'to be pleasing to him'); so 'per piacerli' = 'to please him/it.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g4: 'trassi' is 1st person (past) — an archaic/imperfective past form meaning 'I drew/pulled (out)'; 'de l’acqua' = 'from the water' (archaic contraction 'de' + 'l'' = 'dell''); 'non sazia' is a predicative phrase modifying 'la spugna' ('not satisfying' or 'does not sate'); word order places the modifying predicate after the object: 'I drew from the water the sponge that does not satisfy.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g5: Mossimi is an archaic or poetic fusion of 'mi mossi' (reflexive passato remoto) meaning 'I moved' or 'I stirred myself'. It is a one-word past, reflexive form.
segments.purgatorio-20-g6: 'e' = 'and'. '’l duca mio' = 'my duke/guide' (Dante's leader, here 'duca' used as guide); 'si mosse' is reflexive 3rd person past ('he moved himself' / 'he moved on'). 'per li luoghi spediti' = 'through the free/open/cleared places' (spediti here means open/expeditious), 'pur lungo la roccia' = 'still/indeed along the rock'—a sequence of movement adverbials.
segments.purgatorio-20-g7: 'come si va' is an impersonal construction 'as one goes'; 'per muro stretto' = 'along / by a narrow wall' (stretto = 'narrow'); 'a’ merli' = 'toward the battlements' (merli = battlements/parapet) — the whole simile: they move like someone walking along a narrow wall to the battlements.
segments.purgatorio-20-g8: 'ché' = 'for/because'. 'la gente che fonde' — 'the people who pour / shed' (fonde from 'fondere' here means 'pour out' or 'shed', i.e., tears). 'a goccia a goccia' = 'drop by drop'; 'per li occhi' = 'through their eyes' (i.e., by tears); 'il mal che tutto ’l mondo occupa' = 'the evil/sorrow that occupies/fills the whole world' — a relative clause explaining what they shed.
segments.purgatorio-20-g9: 'da l’altra parte' = 'from the other side'; 'in fuor' is an archaic phrase meaning 'outward' or 'out'; 'troppo s’approccia' = 'approaches/comes too closely' with the reflexive clitic s' (s'approccia, 3rd person present). The clause says those people, from the other side, come out and come too near.
segments.purgatorio-20-g10: 'Maladetta' is an exclamatory adjective 'accursed' or 'damned'. 'sie' is an archaic subjunctive form of 'essere' ('may you be' / 'be'); 'tu' is the emphatic 'you'. Together this is a formulaic curse: 'Accursed be you.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g11: 'antica' = 'ancient' (adjective). 'lupa' literally 'she-wolf' (here used as an epithet); the comma isolates the vocative/exclamation 'ancient she-wolf' addressing that creature.
segments.purgatorio-20-g12: 'che' is the relative pronoun 'who/which'. 'più che' + comparative 'tutte l’altre bestie' = 'more than all the other beasts'. 'hai preda' = present tense 'you have prey' (hai = 2nd person singular of 'avere', 'preda' = prey). 'per la tua fame' = 'because of/through your hunger' (per indicating cause). 'sanza fine' = 'without end' (sanza = archaic 'without'); 'cupa' = 'gloomy/dark' qualifying the hunger. The clause accuses the addressee of having prey because of an endless, gloomy hunger.
segments.purgatorio-20-g13: 'O' is the vocative interjection 'O'/'Oh'. 'ciel' is the poetic form of 'cielo' meaning 'heaven' or 'heavens' and is addressed directly ('O heaven').
segments.purgatorio-20-g14: 'nel' = 'in the'; 'cui' = 'whose' (relative pronoun governing 'girar'); 'girar' = 'turning' or 'rotation' (noun/infinitive used nominally). 'par' = 'it seems'; 'che si creda' is an impersonal/subjunctive construction ('that it is believed') — 'si' marks the impersonal passive and 'creda' is subjunctive because it is subordinated to 'par che'. 'le condizion di qua giù trasmutarsi' = 'that the conditions here below be transformed', where 'trasmutarsi' is a reflexive infinitive 'to be changed/ transmuted.' The whole phrase: 'in whose turning it seems to be believed that the conditions down here are changed.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g15: 'quando' = 'when'; 'verrà' is the future of 'venire' ('will come'); 'per cui' = 'by/through whom' (introducing a relative clause of agency); 'questa' = 'this' (feminine demonstrative, referring back to the condition or situation); 'disceda' is subjunctive of 'discedere' ('to depart/go away') used because the clause is subordinate and contingent — literally: 'when will he come by whom this will depart/cease?'
segments.purgatorio-20-g88: "Perché" here introduces a purpose/result clause and means 'so that'. "men paia" is a literary order where "men" (an archaism for 'meno', 'less') precedes the verb; "paia" is the present subjunctive of parere used impersonally to mean 'may seem' or 'seem less'. "il mal futuro e 'l fatto" are two coordinated noun phrases (the coming evil and the deed/what has been done) functioning as the grammatical subject of the impersonal verb phrase.
segments.purgatorio-20-g89: The verb "veggio" ('I see'/'I perceive') governs an infinitival clause that follows. The preposition "in" plus the infinitive "intrar" gives the sense 'enter into', so word order is inverted for poetic effect: "veggio in Alagna intrar lo fiordaliso" = 'I see the fiordaliso enter into Alagna.' "lo" is the masculine article attached before the noun; "fiordaliso" is a poetic/personified image (here treated as a figure entering the place named Alagna).
segments.purgatorio-20-g90: The conjunction "e" links this clause to the previous vision. "nel vicario suo" = 'in/within his vicar' ("nel" = in + article). The infinitive clause "Cristo esser catto" is a subordinate infinitive complement of "veggio"; "esser" is the infinitive 'to be' and "catto" here is an opaque epithet/name applied to Cristo (an unusual/loaded word used as a label rather than a normal adjective).
segments.purgatorio-20-g91: The form "Veggiolo" is best read as the verb "veggio" with the object clitic "-lo" attached (i.e. 'I see him'); the text spells it together for metrical reasons. "un'altra volta" = 'another time' or 'again.' The infinitive "esser deriso" is a passive infinitive 'to be mocked' and functions as the complement of the verb 'veggio' (I see him again to be mocked).
segments.purgatorio-20-g92: "veggio" again introduces what is seen; here it governs the infinitive "rinovellar" ('to renew'). The direct objects "l'aceto e 'l fiele" ('the vinegar and the gall') follow the verb; both are used metaphorically for renewed bitterness. Note the enclitic article "l'" before each noun.
segments.purgatorio-20-g93: The conjunction "e" continues the list of visions. "tra" means 'among' and governs the noun phrase "vivi ladroni" ('living thieves' or 'thieves who are alive'). The infinitive "esser anciso" ('to be slain/struck down') is again the complement of "veggio"; "anciso" is a past participle used in a passive infinitive construction.
segments.purgatorio-20-g94: "Veggio" ('I see'/'I foresee') introduces the vision. "il novo Pilato" is a noun phrase 'the new Pilate' and "sì crudele" is an intensifying adverb + adjective ('so cruel'). Word order places the intensifier after the noun for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-20-g95: "che" is a relative pronoun meaning 'who/which/that' and introduces a subordinate clause describing Pilate; "ciò" is a demonstrative 'that' or 'this.' "nol" is the contracted negative "non lo" ('does not it/that'), where "lo" refers back to the thing mentioned, and "sazia" is present ('satisfies'/'satiates'), so the clause reads 'which/that does not satisfy him.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g96: The adversative "ma" ('but') introduces the contrasting action. "sanza" is the archaic form of 'senza' ('without') and governs the noun "decreto" ('decree'). The infinitive "portar" ('to carry/bring') takes the prepositional phrase "nel Tempio" ('into the Temple') and the direct object "le cupide vele" ('the covetous/greedy sails'); "cupide" is an adjective meaning 'covetous/greedy' modifying "vele" ('sails') used metaphorically.
segments.purgatorio-20-g97: A direct apostrophe. O is the exclamatory particle 'O/Oh'; Segnor mio = 'my Lord' (address to God or a lordly figure), with mio as the possessive 'my.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g98: This is a future-time question. quando sarò io lieto a veder = 'when shall I be glad/happy to see' — sarò is future of essere ('I will be'), io is emphatic subject, lieto = 'glad', a + infinitive (a veder) expresses about/at seeing or 'to see'; la vendetta che introduces the relative clause describing what will be seen.
segments.purgatorio-20-g99: nascosa is an adjective modifying vendetta: 'hidden' or 'secret' — placed parenthetically to qualify the vengeance.
segments.purgatorio-20-g100: fa dolce l’ira tua nel tuo secreto?: fa + adjective + direct object = 'makes your wrath sweet' (fa = makes); dolce = 'sweet/pleasant'; l’ira tua = 'your anger'; nel tuo secreto = 'in your secret/hidden counsels' (prepositional phrase locating where that sweetness occurs). The rhetorical effect contrasts hidden divine justice with the sweetness of divine wrath in God's secret plan.
segments.purgatorio-20-g29: Esso is an emphatic/archaic third-person pronoun ('he' or 'it') referring back to the spirit; parlava is the imperfect (3rd singular) of parlare, 'was speaking' or 'spoke on an ongoing basis'; ancor is an archaic form of ancora, 'still'; de is the older poetic form of di used here as 'about' or 'of'; la larghezza literally 'the largeness' is used idiomatically as 'the largesse/generosity'; che introduces a relative clause; fece is passato remoto (3rd singular) of fare, 'he did/made' (here 'showed' or 'displayed'); Niccolò is the proper name; a le pulcelle = a + definite article + noun ('to the maidens'), with the older separate a + le rather than modern alle.
segments.purgatorio-20-g30: Per introduces purpose ('in order to'); condurre is the infinitive 'to lead/bring'; ad is the preposition 'to/toward' before a noun; onor (poetic truncation of onore) means 'honor' or 'good name'; lor is the enclitic archaic form of loro ('their') placed after the noun for metrical reasons; giovinezza is 'youth' — the phrase means 'to bring their youth to honor' or 'to lead their youthful years into honor.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g31: O is the vocative interjection 'O/Oh'; anima is the vocative 'soul' being addressed; che is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing the clause modifying anima; tanto + ben is an idiom meaning 'so well' or 'with such excellence'; favelle is an archaic verb form (favellare) here meaning 'speaks' or 'is eloquent' — the form favelle ends in -e in older Tuscan usage.
segments.purgatorio-20-g32: Dimmi is the imperative 'tell me' (di' + mi); chi is the interrogative 'who'; fosti is the passato remoto (second-person singular) of essere, 'you were' — the question asks the soul to identify who she was in life.
segments.purgatorio-20-g33: Dissi is the first-person passato remoto of dire, 'I said' — a simple narrative past marking the speaker's utterance in the poem's historical present narration.
segments.purgatorio-20-g34: E = 'and'; perché here is 'why' (introducing the question); sola = 'alone' or 'only'; tu is the emphatic subject pronoun 'you' placed before the verb for emphasis; queste degne lode = 'these worthy praises' (queste modifying lode); rinovelle is an archaic verb form (Dante's spelling) meaning 'renews' or 'is renewing' — the form -e is an older inflection and the verb is used with tu as the addressed subject, so the sense is 'why do you alone renew these worthy praises.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g35: Non = 'not'; fia is an archaic/fixed-form future of essere (3rd singular) meaning 'will be' (modern sarà) used here in an assertive future; sanza is the older form of senza, 'without'; mercé = mercé/mercede, 'mercy' or 'favor'; la tua parola 'your word' (the subject follows for metrical order).
segments.purgatorio-20-g36: S’io is a contraction of se io ('if I'); ritorno is present (or historic present) 'I return' or 'I come back'; a compiér is 'to complete' (compiér is the infinitive, spelled with an acute for the stressed syllable in the manuscript tradition); lo cammin corto = 'the short way/journey' (lo + noun; corto = 'short'); di quella vita = 'of that life'; ch’ (che) introduces a relative clause; al (a + il) termine = 'to/at the end'; vola is present tense of volare, here figuratively 'flies' or 'hastens' — the whole conditional clause means 'if I return to finish the short path of that life which flies to its end.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g37: Ed is the connective 'and' (poetic form of e); elli is an archaic pronoun used as 'he' (the speaker) — Dante frequently uses older pronouns for meter and style.
segments.purgatorio-20-g38: Io ti dirò: Io 'I' + ti 'to you' + dirò, future indicative of dire 'I will tell (you)' — a straightforward future-tense promise to answer.
segments.purgatorio-20-g39: Non per conforto = 'not for the sake of comfort' (per expresses purpose/reason here); ch’io attenda di là: ch’ (che) introduces a subordinate clause, io is subject 'I', attenda is present-subjunctive or present of attendere used here with the nuance 'I expect' or 'I look for' (Dante often uses subjunctive in subordinate clauses), di là = 'there' or 'beyond' (i.e., in the afterlife or elsewhere).
segments.purgatorio-20-g40: Ma = 'but'; perché = 'because'; tanta = adverbial/quantifier 'so great'; grazia = 'grace' or 'favor' (spiritual gift); in te = 'in you' (locative); luce is present of lucere 'shines' (used impersonally/actively of grace shining in someone); prima che sie morto = 'before you are dead' — che introduces the temporal subordinate clause and sie is an archaic subjunctive/present of essere 'be' used with morto 'dead'.
segments.purgatorio-20-g74: Sanz' is a contracted, poetic form of senza ('without'). arme is the plural noun 'arms/weapons'. n’esce represents ne + esce ('comes out of it' or 'emerges from it'); here ne is the clitic indicating source, and esce is the 3rd‑person singular present of uscire. e solo con la lancia is a coordinated phrase: e 'and', solo 'alone/only' modifies the following prepositional phrase con la lancia ('with the lance').
segments.purgatorio-20-g75: e quella ponta sì, links the preceding clause to a following result clause: e 'and'; quella 'that (one)'; ponta is an archaic spelling of punta ('point/tip') functioning as a noun; sì here is an adverb 'so' (intensifier) that prepares the following consequence.
segments.purgatorio-20-g76: ch’ is the apocopated che introducing a result/explanatory clause ('so that/so...that'); a Fiorenza uses the preposition a with the place name Fiorenza ('at/in Florence'); fa scoppiar is the causative construction: fa (3rd‑sg. of fare) + infinitive scoppiar (poetic infinitive of scoppiare) meaning 'causes to burst'; la pancia is the direct object 'the belly' (here figuratively: the city’s guts/wealth).
segments.purgatorio-20-g77: Quindi is an adverb indicating result/consequence ('therefore/from this'); non negates the noun that follows; terra is a noun 'land/territory' and here is the (negated) thing he will not gain — the verb is postponed to the next segment (elliptical construction).
segments.purgatorio-20-g78: ma introduces the contrast 'but'; peccato e onta are coordinated nouns 'sin and shame' (onta = archaic 'dishonor'); guadagnerà is the future indicative, 3rd‑person singular of guadagnare ('will gain/earn'), and supplies the verb omitted in the previous segment, taking peccato e onta as its objects.
segments.purgatorio-20-g79: per sé is a dative/benefactive phrase 'for himself'; tanto più grave expresses a comparative degree 'all the more serious' (tanto più = 'so much the more'); grave is an adjective agreeing with the understood the thing gained (peccato e onta) and comments on its weight for him.
segments.purgatorio-20-g80: quanto più … conta forms a correlative comparative: quanto più ... più ... ('the more ... the more ...'). quanto here introduces the measure clause ('the more as'), più 'more', lieve 'light/slight' modifies danno ('damage/harm'), and conta is the 3rd‑person singular present of contare used in the sense 'is reckoned/ counts' — i.e. the less serious the damage is regarded, the worse the guilt for him.
segments.purgatorio-20-g81: L’ altro is the simple noun phrase 'the other' (L' = il + elision). The comma marks that what follows will be a relative clause identifying this 'other.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g82: che introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; già 'already'; uscì is the passato remoto (3rd‑singular) of uscire 'he went out/left'; preso is a past participle 'taken/captured' modifying the subject; di nave literally 'from the ship' ('captured from the ship' or 'taken off the ship').
segments.purgatorio-20-g83: veggio is the 1st‑person present of vedere used here in a prophetic or visionary sense ('I see' / 'I foresee'); vender is the infinitive 'to sell' and is the first verb of the coordinated infinitival predicate; sua figlia is the direct object 'his daughter'; e patteggiarne is the second coordinated infinitive 'and to negotiate/settle about her' — the enclitic ne on patteggiar‑ refers back to figlia (i.e. 'bargain over her'); come fanno i corsar de l’altre schiave is a simile 'as corsairs do with other slaves.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g84: O is the vocative/exclamatory particle 'Oh'; avarizia is the vocative noun 'avarice/greed' addressed directly. The phrase is an apostrophe to the abstract vice.
segments.purgatorio-20-g85: che introduces a rhetorical question 'what'; puoi is the 2nd‑person singular present of potere 'can/are you able'; tu is the emphatic subject 'you'; più here means 'any more' or 'further' (i.e. 'what more can you do'); farne is fare + ne (infinitive + clitic) meaning 'to do of it / to make of it' (what more can you do with it).
segments.purgatorio-20-g86: poscia is an adverb 'after/ since/ afterwards' giving the temporal/causal frame; c’ ha is the contracted ci + ha (have) construction — here indicating possession or relation ('has to/for you'); il mio sangue 'my blood' is the object; a te 'to you' (dative); sì 'so' intensifies; tratto is a past participle used adjectivally or as predicate ('drawn'), so the whole phrase means 'since you have drawn my blood to you so much.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g87: che begins the result clause 'that'; non si cura is a pronominal reflexive construction with curare ('does not care for/attend to') — non negates the verb, si is reflexive/impersonal; de la propria carne 'of its own flesh' (de = of, la propria = its own, carne = flesh/body) expresses the thing neglected. The whole is a rhetorical question about the corrupting power just described.
segments.purgatorio-20-g58: Mentre che functions as a subordinating conjunction meaning "while"; the phrase that follows is the subordinate clause. The grammatical subject is la gran dota provenzale (noun phrase) and the finite verb is tolse, passato remoto (3rd person singular) of togliere. The negative particle non precedes the verb; al sangue mio is a prepositional phrase (a + il = al) marking whose blood/lineage is meant and la vergogna is the direct object of tolse. Poetic inversion places the verb before the object for rhythm.
segments.purgatorio-20-g59: Poco is an adverb meaning "little/not much" and precedes the verb valea. Valea is an archaic/imperfect or poetic form of valere here meaning "was worth" or "had value"; the clause states that it had little worth.
segments.purgatorio-20-g60: Ma pur non facea male is a contrastive clause: ma = "but"; pur (from pure/pur) = "still/nevertheless"; non + facea (imperfect of fare) gives a negated imperfect meaning "was not doing/causing"; male is a noun/adverb meaning "harm/evils," so the phrase means "yet it did no harm."
segments.purgatorio-20-g61: Lì sets the locative "there." Cominciò is passato remoto (3rd person singular) of cominciare "began." The instrument phrase con forza e con menzogna uses con + nouns to indicate means/way (with force and with deception). La sua rapina is the noun phrase (the robbery/plunder) and appears after the verb by poetic inversion (the verb 'began' governs that noun phrase).
segments.purgatorio-20-g62: E poscia is a simple connective: e = "and," poscia (archaic/poetic) = "afterwards/then." It links the previous clause to what follows.
segments.purgatorio-20-g63: Per ammenda is a short adverbial phrase meaning "as amends/for compensation/ by way of reparation." Per introduces the role or purpose and ammenda is a noun meaning "amends/atonement."
segments.purgatorio-20-g64: This is a verbless series continued from the previous clause: prese is passato remoto (3rd person singular) of prendere "took/seized." The names Pontì, Normandia and Guascogna are the objects taken; Italian frequently lists coordinated objects with repeated e (and) for cadence here.
segments.purgatorio-20-g65: Carlo is the subject (proper name). Venne is passato remoto of venire (3rd person singular) meaning "came/arrived." In Italia is the locative complement. The coordinating e links to the following remark.
segments.purgatorio-20-g66: Per ammenda repeats the adverbial phrase "as amends/for reparation," with per introducing the purpose or motive and ammenda as the noun.
segments.purgatorio-20-g67: Vittima fé di Curradino is an instance of inverted or compact word order: vittima (a victim) appears before the finite verb fé (archaic spelling of fece, passato remoto of fare = "made"). Di Curradino marks the person affected ("of Curradino" = "a victim of Curradino"), so the whole means "he made Curradino a victim."
segments.purgatorio-20-g68: E poi = "and then." Ripinse is an archaic/poetic past form (here used figuratively) — lemma given is ripingere but the context uses it to mean the act of sending or restoring; it is the finite verb in passato remoto. Al ciel is the directional phrase "to heaven" (a + il = al) and Tommaso is the person placed after the prepositional phrase by inversion (object placed postpositively for emphasis).
segments.purgatorio-20-g69: Per ammenda closes the sentence again with the same purposive phrase "as amends."
segments.purgatorio-20-g70: Tempo is the direct object/accusative notion 'a time/period.' Vegg' is an elided archaic form of vedere (veggio/vedo) meaning "I see"; the apostrophe marks the contraction before the pronoun io, which follows the verb (subject pronoun placed after the verb for emphasis: vegg' io = "I see").
segments.purgatorio-20-g71: Non molto dopo ancoi is a temporal adverbial: non molto dopo = "not long after" and ancoi (archaic) reinforces the immediacy ('still/now/shortly thereafter'). The negatives and adverbs precede the temporal noun.
segments.purgatorio-20-g72: Che introduces a subordinate clause ('when/that'). Tragge is present (archaic form of traggere) meaning "draws/leads" and is the finite verb; un altro Carlo is the subject. Fuor di Francia is a locative/partitive phrase meaning "out of/from France" (fuor = fuori). Word order is normal for an independent subordinate clause.
segments.purgatorio-20-g73: Per + infinitive expresses purpose: per far conoscer = "in order to make known" (far + infinitive is a causative/auxiliary construction). Meglio modifies conoscer ("better"). E sé e ' suoi is a balanced coordinate phrase "both himself and his own" (sé is a reflexive pronoun stressed by e...e, and 'suoi stands for his followers/men).
segments.purgatorio-20-g41: Io fui radice de la mala pianta che la terra cristiana tutta aduggia is a declarative clause with a predicative nominal construction: the past-tense verb fui (from essere) plus the noun radice form the core 'I was root'. De is the older preposition equivalent to modern di ('of/from'). The relative che introduces a relative clause modifying pianta; word order places the relative clause after the noun as in modern Italian. Aduggia is a poetical/archaic verb meaning 'overshadows/darkens' and takes la terra cristiana tutta as its object (the whole Christian world).
segments.purgatorio-20-g42: Sì che begins a result clause 'so that / therefore'. Buon frutto is the noun phrase 'good fruit/result'. Rado is used adverbially ('rarely' or 'seldom') and appears before the verb for emphasis. Se ne schianta is an older/pronominal construction: se (here reflexive/impersonal si in older orthography) + ne ('of it/from it') + schianta (3rd-person singular of schiantare) yields 'seldom springs/comes from it' or 'rarely results from it'.
segments.purgatorio-20-g43: Ma se is a simple adversative + conditional particle ('but if'). Doagio is a place-name in the series of cities; the clause is the protasis (the 'if' part) of a conditional sentence and so uses se to introduce an unreal/possible action.
segments.purgatorio-20-g44: Lilla is simply one item in the enumerated list of cities. The segment is a coordination pause; punctuation and placement serve the poem's rhythm.
segments.purgatorio-20-g45: Guanto e Bruggia potesser is the continuation: the coordinate Guanto e Bruggia ('Guanto and Bruges') followed by potesser, an archaic subjunctive/conditional form corresponding to modern potessero or potessero/possere 'could/would be able'. Potesser marks the conditional ability of those cities to act (elliptical object: 'do something about it').
segments.purgatorio-20-g46: Tosto ne saria vendetta is the apodosis (result): tosto ('quickly/at once') + ne ('of it/from it') + saria (conditional of essere, 'there would be') + vendetta ('revenge/vengeance'). The construction saria vendetta is impersonal: 'there would be vengeance for it.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g47: E io la cheggio a lui che tutto giuggia: e ('and') + io (subject) + la (the thing—'it', direct object referring to 'vendetta') + cheggio (first-person singular present of chiedere in an irregular/poetic form, 'I ask/plead for') + a lui ('to him') + che ('who') + tutto giuggia (a clause qualifying 'him': 'who ... all ...' where giuggia is a poetic/archaic verb meaning something like 'judges/rules' here). The whole clause means 'and I ask it of Him who judges/governs all.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g48: Chiamato fui is a passive/perfect construction with participle + auxiliary in the past (chiamato fui = 'I was called' or 'I have been called'). Di là ('from there/over there') indicates location. The proper name Ugo Ciappetta follows, so the whole line means 'Over there I was called Ugo Ciappetta.' Word order (participle + fui + subject) is an emphatic/poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-20-g49: Di me son nati i Filippi e i Luigi is a causative/origination statement: di me ('from me') + son nati (literal present perfect/poetic present 'have been born' or 'sprang from me') + i Filippi e i Luigi (the dynastic names). Per cui novellamente è Francia retta is a relative/result clause: per cui ('by whom/through whom') + novellamente ('recently/newly') + è retta ('is ruled/governed') — retta is an adjective/past participle used predicatively ('governed'). Together: 'From me sprang the Philippes and the Louis, by whom France has lately been governed.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g50: Figliuol fu' io d'un beccaio di Parigi uses fronted noun Figliuol ('son') followed by the auxiliary fu' (poetic past of essere) and the subject pronoun io — a poetic inversion meaning 'I was a son of a butcher of Paris.' D'un (= di un) introduces origin/parentage.
segments.purgatorio-20-g51: Quando li regi antichi venner meno tutti is a temporal clause: quando ('when') + li regi antichi ('the ancient/former kings') + venner meno (‘vennero meno’ in modern spelling — an idiom meaning 'fell away', 'declined', 'ceased') + tutti ('all'). The verb venner meno is an older compound expressing diminution or failure of power.
segments.purgatorio-20-g52: Fuor ch'un renduto in panni bigi is an exception clause: fuor che ('except that') + un renduto ('one returned' or 'one who had come back') + in panni bigi ('in gray garments'). The adjective bigi = gray; the whole phrase qualifies the exception to 'all' in the prior clause.
segments.purgatorio-20-g53: Trova'mi stretto ne le mani il freno del governo del regno is built around the archaic clitic inversion trova' mi ('I found myself' or 'I found') + stretto ('tight/held fast'): 'I found the rein tightly in my hands.' Ne le mani = 'in (or into) the hands' (ne used with physical/verbal phrases) and il freno del governo del regno is the object: 'the bridle/rein of the realm's government.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g54: E tanta possa di nuovo acquisto links results: e ('and') + tanta possa ('so great a power') + di nuovo acquisto ('from/through new acquisition' — i.e. newly gained wealth or influence). Poss(a) here is a substantive meaning 'power/ability' rather than a present-subjunctive form.
segments.purgatorio-20-g55: E sì d'amici pieno continues the listing of advantages: e ('and') + sì ('so/indeed') + d'amici ('of friends' = 'with friends') + pieno ('full'). The phrase means 'and so full of friends' (i.e. surrounded by supporters).
segments.purgatorio-20-g56: Ch'a la corona vedova promossa la testa di mio figlio fu is a result clause with poetic word order: ch' (that) + a la corona vedova promossa ('promoted/raised to the widow's crown' — promossa functions as a participle/adjective placed before its logical subject for metrical reasons) + la testa di mio figlio fu ('the head of my son was' — i.e. my son was raised to the crown). The auxiliary fu is the past tense 'was'.
segments.purgatorio-20-g57: Dal quale cominciar di costor le sacrate ossa is a relative/result phrase: dal quale ('from whom' / 'from which') + cominciar (infinitive used elliptically for 'began'/'are begun') + di costor ('of these people') + le sacrate ossa ('the sacred bones' — i.e. their holy remains or ancestry). Syntactically the phrase indicates that from that person (dal quale) the 'sacred bones' of these people begin (their line/origin).
segments.purgatorio-20-g135: Poi is an adverb meaning "then/afterwards." Cominciò is the passato remoto 3rd-singular of cominciare ("began"); the verb precedes the subject here. Da tutte parti is an adverbial phrase "from all sides" and un grido tal (tale shortened to tal, poetic) is the subject phrase "such a cry."
segments.purgatorio-20-g136: Che introduces a result/relative clause "such that." 'l is the elided definite article (il). Maestro is the leader/guide (Virgil). Inverso me is an older locution meaning "turned toward me/facing me." Si feo is an archaic past reflexive construction (si + feo, an old form of feci/fèce) meaning "made himself/turned himself" — i.e. "he turned toward me."
segments.purgatorio-20-g137: Dicendo is the gerund of dire and links the preceding action to the following direct speech: "saying:" (it marks the manner in which he turned toward the speaker).
segments.purgatorio-20-g138: Non is the negative particle "not." Dubbiar is an archaic form of the verb (dubitare) used here as an imperative (second-person singular) meaning "do not doubt." The final -e is dropped in the poetic form.
segments.purgatorio-20-g139: Mentr' is the clitic form of mentre "while." Io is emphatic subject "I." Ti is the object pronoun "you." Guido is present indicative first-person of guidare: "I guide/lead you." Together: "while I guide you."
segments.purgatorio-20-g140: The liturgical phrase Glorïa in excelsis Deo is quoted but split by Dante's syntax; tutti modifies the speakers ("they all"). Dicean is the imperfect 3rd-person plural of dire (they were saying/kept saying), a narrative tense describing a continuous or repeated utterance.
segments.purgatorio-20-g141: Per quel ch' introduces a clause meaning "by/through that which/what." Io is the subject "I." Da' vicin is a compact expression "from the nearby ones/people beside me" (da + vicin). Compresi is passato remoto first-person of comprendere "I understood/was able to grasp."
segments.purgatorio-20-g142: Onde here functions as 'whence'/'so that' introducing the result: "from which/so that..." Intender in this context means "to understand/hear" the cry; lo grido is the direct object "the cry." Si poteo is an archaic/periphrastic form: si (reflexive/impersonal particle) + poteo (past form meaning "I was able") — together indicating that the speaker was able to perceive/understand it.
segments.purgatorio-20-g143: No' here is an archaic form representing the subject "noi" (we) in Dante's orthography. Istavamo is the imperfect of istare (we were/stood). Immobili e sospesi are predicate adjectives "motionless and suspended/held in suspense." Come introduces a simile: i pastor (the shepherds) che prima udir quel canto = "who first heard that song." Udir is the archaic infinitive "to hear."
segments.purgatorio-20-g144: Fin che means "until." 'l tremar uses the infinitive tremar as a noun with the article ("the trembling"). Cessò is passato remoto 3rd-singular "ceased." Ed is a variant of e "and." El is an archaic pronoun/article (it) and compiési is passato remoto first-person of compiere "I completed" — so "and I completed it."
segments.purgatorio-20-g145: Poi "then." Ripigliammo is passato remoto first-person plural of ripigliare: "we resumed/took up again." Nostro cammin santo is the noun phrase "our holy path/journey" (possessive + noun + adjective in normal order).
segments.purgatorio-20-g146: Guardando is the gerund "looking at" modifying the main action. L'ombre (the elided article + ombre) "the shades/shadows" is the direct object of the gerund; che giacean is a relative clause with giacean, the imperfect of giacere (they lay/layed) describing their position, and per terra means "on the ground."
segments.purgatorio-20-g147: Tornate is the past participle (feminine plural) agreeing with ombre: "returned." Già "already." In su l'usato pianto: in su has a sense of 'back to' or 'up into,' l'usato pianto is "the customary/old weeping" — the shades have returned to their usual lament.
segments.purgatorio-20-g121: This segment contains only punctuation (a single period) and carries no lexical or grammatical content to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-20-g122: Talor is an adverb ('sometimes'); parla is present indicative 'speaks' (here general/habitual); the phrase l’uno ... l’altro places the two contrasted subjects in the definite form (l' = il) and uses adjectives alto / basso to describe manner of speaking (literally 'high' and 'low', i.e. loud and quiet). Word order (subject omitted) is idiomatic: 'Sometimes one speaks high and the other low.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g123: secondo introduces a phrase meaning 'according to'; l’affezion is a noun meaning an inclination or affection that controls behavior; ch' is the clitic of che ('that'); ad ir uses the infinitive ir ('to go') with the clitic ci ('us') as its indirect object; sprona is present indicative 'spurs/urges'. ora ... ora is a repeated adverbial expression 'now ... now ...' with a + comparative (a maggiore / a minor passo) indicating alternation of faster and slower pace.
segments.purgatorio-20-g124: però marks a contrast ('therefore/but'); al ben is a prepositional phrase 'with respect to the good' (al = a + il); che introduces a relative clause; '’l dì' here is the phrase for 'what is said' or 'the saying' (poetic use of dì from dire), ci si ragiona uses the impersonal/reciprocal si with ci ('to/for us') and ragiona (discusses/reasons), so the clause means roughly 'that which is discussed to us here.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g125: dianzi is an adverb 'formerly/before'; non + era (imperfect of essere) negates the state 'I was not'; io is the subject pronoun (emphatic here); sol (solo) means 'alone' or 'sole'. The imperfect era with dianzi sets this as a prior, continuous state: 'previously I was not alone.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g126: ma introduces contrast ('but'); qui da presso is a locative phrase 'here, close by'; non + alzava (imperfect of alzare) means 'no one raised' or 'was not raising'; la voce altra persona places the object ('the voice') before the subject ('another person') for emphasis: literally 'no other person raised the voice.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g127: Noi is the subject 'we'; eravam is the archaic/poetic imperfect (eravamo) of essere used with the past participle partiti to form a compound past sense ('we had departed' / 'we were departed'); già reinforces 'already'; da esso is a prepositional phrase 'from it.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g128: e brigavam uses the imperfect brigavamo ('we were striving' / 'we strove continually'); di + infinitive soverchiar expresses the aim ('to overtake/surpass'); la strada is the object; tanto quanto introduces a limit clause 'as much as' and al poder n'era permesso is a passive/impersonal construction meaning 'as far as to the power it was permitted' — the clitic n' (ne) refers back to the extent and era permesso is 'was permitted.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g129: quand’ is the elided form of quando ('when'); io is the subject pronoun for emphasis; senti’ is the elided past tense (sentì, passato remoto) of sentire, here best rendered 'I heard' or 'I felt' depending on context — the verb introduces what he perceived immediately afterward.
segments.purgatorio-20-g130: come introduces a simile 'as/like'; cosa 'thing' fills the subject slot of the simile; che introduces a subordinate clause; cada is the present subjunctive of cadere (poetic form used where English uses 'were to fall' or 'should fall'), so the whole phrase reads 'as if something were falling.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g131: tremar is the infinitive 'to tremble' used as the thing perceived by senti' ('I heard [the] mountain tremble'); lo monte places the definite article before the noun (Dante frequently uses lo for euphony) to mean 'the mountain.' The infinitive here functions as a simple object/complement of senti'.
segments.purgatorio-20-g132: onde ('whence' / 'thereupon') introduces the result; mi prese is a simple past 'seized me' (prese = prese, passato remoto of prendere); un gelo 'a chill' names the sensation; qual introduces a simile 'such as'; prender suol is a verbal phrase where suol (from suolare) expresses habitual action ('is wont to take'); colui ch’a morte vada is a relative clause 'he who is going to death' (vada = present subjunctive of andare used in a relative/relative-optative sense), giving the comparison 'such as one is wont to feel who goes to his death.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g133: Certo here is an adverb/adjectival intensifier 'certainly' or 'surely'; non si scoteo is a negative past ('did not shake itself' or 'was not shaken') with the reflexive si; scoteo is a poetic/archaic past form of scotere/scuotere meaning 'shook'; sì forte intensifies the manner 'so strongly/violently'; Delo (Delos) is the grammatical subject placed at the end for emphasis (proper noun, island).
segments.purgatorio-20-g134: pria che introduces a temporal clause 'before'; Latona is the subject (Leto of mythology); in lei ('in it/her') refers back to Delo (the island, feminine in Italian); facesse is the imperfect subjunctive of fare ('should/might make' / 'had made'), used here in the subordinate temporal clause to mean 'before Leto made'; ’l nido is 'the nest' (l' = il); a parturir is an infinitive expressing purpose 'to give birth to'; li due occhi del cielo is a poetic apposition 'the two eyes of the sky/heaven' (i.e. the twin children), with li = the definite article and del = di + il.
segments.purgatorio-20-g148: Nulla ignoranza is the subject phrase ('no kind of ignorance' / 'never any ignorance') followed by the adverb mai for emphasis. Con tanta guerra is a prepositional phrase ('with such a struggle') modifying the verb. Fé is an archaic (Dantean) form of the passato remoto (3rd sing.) of fare meaning 'made/caused'; mi is the clitic object 'me', and desideroso di + infinitive is a predicate adjective construction (‘desirous to know’).
segments.purgatorio-20-g149: Se introduces a conditional 'if'. La memoria mia is the subject ('my memory'); in ciò is a locative/abstract prepositional complement ('in this/that') modifying erra. Erra is present indicative of errare, here idiomatically 'be mistaken' ('does not err').
segments.purgatorio-20-g150: Quanta is an interrogative/exclamatory determiner 'how much' modifying the verb. Pareami is an enclitic form (mi attached to parea) and is the imperfect/parallelistic form of parere meaning 'it seemed to me' or 'I seemed to'. Allor is the temporal adverb 'then'.
segments.purgatorio-20-g151: Pensando is a gerund ('thinking') functioning parenthetically to qualify the speaker's state while he judged how much he had; it links the mental action to the preceding verb 'pareami'.
segments.purgatorio-20-g152: Avere is the bare infinitive complement of the earlier desideroso di sapere … quanta … avere — 'to have'. The semicolon (in the original) separates this object clause from the following negative clauses.
segments.purgatorio-20-g153: Né = 'nor'. Per la fretta is causal ('because of / through haste'). Dimandare is the infinitive 'to ask' placed before the finite verb; er' is a contracted archaic form of ero (1st person imperfect of essere) and oso is an adjectival/predicative form from osare ('daring') — together meaning 'I was daring (to ask)' so the whole phrase reads 'nor, for haste, was I daring to ask' (i.e. I did not dare).
segments.purgatorio-20-g154: Né again 'nor'. Per me lì is 'for me there' or 'there, for me' marking the experiencer and place. Potea is an imperfect form of potere ('could / was able to') with an implied subject 'I'; cosa vedere = 'to see anything' (cosa indefinite). The colon closes the negative series.
segments.purgatorio-20-g155: Così 'thus/so' introduces the resulting state. M'andava is the enclitic form of mi + andava (imperfect of andare) meaning 'I went on / I was going' in the sense 'I proceeded' or 'I behaved'; timido e pensoso are predicate adjectives describing his manner ('timid and pensive').
segments.purgatorio-20-g101: Ciò ch’io dicea ... introduces a relative/free‑relative clause meaning “what I was saying.” The contraction ch’ = che (relative 'that/which') links Ciò to io dicea (dicea = imperfect indicative, “I was saying”). The phrase di quell’ unica sposa de lo Spirito Santo is a prepositional phrase (de lo = archaic form of del) modifying what was said. In e che ti fece verso me volger per alcuna chiosa, fece is the perfect/simple past causative “made/caused,” with the clitic ti as its object and the infinitive volger (“to turn”) as the complement; per alcuna chiosa means “for some brief retort/explanation.”
segments.purgatorio-20-g102: tanto è risposto a tutte nostre prece quanto ’l dì dura uses a correlative tanto ... quanto meaning roughly “to the extent/as long as.” È risposto is a stative/passive construction (essere + past participle) meaning “is answered” or “finds reply,” followed by the indirect object a tutte nostre prece (“to all our prayers”). Quanto ’l dì dura = “as long as the day lasts” (’l = il; dura = present indicative).
segments.purgatorio-20-g103: ma com’ el s’annotta, is a short temporal clause: ma = but; com’ (come) = as/when; el = “it” (referring back to il dì); s’annotta is a pronominal/archaic verb meaning “becomes night” or “night falls” (s’ is the reflexive/clitic pronoun). The clause signals a change of circumstance when night comes.
segments.purgatorio-20-g104: contrario suon prendemo in quella vece states the result: contrari(o) suon = “an opposite tone/sound”; prendemo is first‑person plural present (archaic -emo ending) meaning “we take/adopt”; in quella vece = “on that occasion/in that turn.” Together: “we adopt an opposite tone on that occasion.”
segments.purgatorio-20-g105: Noi is the subject 'we'. repetiam is an archaic present form meaning 'we repeat' (modern repetiamo); Pigmalïon is the direct object (the mythic figure); allotta is an adverb meaning 'thereupon/then' placed after the verb for rhythmic effect.
segments.purgatorio-20-g106: cui is the relative 'to whom' (referring back to Pigmalïon). The three nouns 'traditore e ladro e paricida' are predicative nouns describing what he became; fece is the past (simple) of fare used causatively 'made (him)'; la voglia sua de l’oro ghiotta = 'his desire for gold, greedy' where de lo = archaic 'del', and ghiotta modifies voglia ('greedy/insatiable'). Word order (predicatives before fece) is emphatic and typical of poetic style.
segments.purgatorio-20-g107: e is the conjunction 'and'. la miseria = 'the misery' (here meaning the wretched state or moral ruin). de l’avaro Mida = 'of the avaricious Midas' (de + article = archaic 'del'; avaro characterizes Mida as greedy). The phrase names another example of greed's consequences.
segments.purgatorio-20-g108: che = 'who/that' introducing a relative clause about Mida. seguì is the past 'followed/ensued' (he followed/pursued); a la sua dimanda = 'his request' (dimanda = request); gorda 'fat' figuratively = 'greedy/large' (i.e. a self-indulgent request). The construction means 'who followed his greedy request' (he obeyed/acted on it).
segments.purgatorio-20-g109: per la qual = 'for which' (relative reason), sempre 'always'. convien is an impersonal present 'it is necessary / fitting'; che si rida is a subordinate with impersonal si and the subjunctive rida = 'that one laugh' (modern: conviene che si rida). Thus 'for which one must always laugh.' Note: rida here is the subjunctive of 'ridere' (to laugh).
segments.purgatorio-20-g110: Del = contraction di + il 'of the/about the'. folle Acàn = epithet + name 'the foolish Achan'. ciascun 'each one/everyone' is singular and governs the reflexive si ricorda; poi = 'then/afterwards'. si ricorda is reflexive 'remembers' used with ciascun to mean 'each one remembers.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g111: come introduces an explanatory 'how' clause (how he acted). furò is the past of furare 'he stole' (remote past). le spoglie = 'the spoils' (plunder) as the direct object. The clause recounts his theft.
segments.purgatorio-20-g112: sì che = 'so that', introducing a result clause. l’ira di Iosüè = 'the wrath of Joshua' (subject). qui 'here/in this matter'. par = present 'seems'. ch’ ancor lo morda = 'that still (ancor) bites/gnaws him': par + che + subjunctive morda expresses 'it appears that ...'; lo is the accusative clitic 'him' (referring back to Achan). Morda is present subjunctive of mordere used figuratively 'gnaw at/torment'.
segments.purgatorio-20-g113: Indi = 'then' or 'thence'. accusiam is an archaic first-person plural present 'we accuse' (modern accusiamo). col = contraction of con + il 'with the' (here 'together with'); marito Saffira = 'the husband of Sapphira' (or 'Sapphira's husband') — the phrase accuses Sapphira along with her husband.
segments.purgatorio-20-g114: lodiam is an archaic form meaning 'we praise' or 'let us praise' used ironically here. i calci 'the kicks' (literal blows) is the object. ch’ (che) introduces the relative clause 'that he received'; ebbe is the past of avere 'he had/received' (used here as 'he got'); Elïodoro is the person named.
segments.purgatorio-20-g115: e = 'and'. in infamia = 'into/into a state of infamy/disgrace'. tutto ’l monte gira = 'the whole mountain turns' (gira = present 'turns/moves about'); the following name (Polinestòr...) is what the mountain turns upon — i.e. the object of the disgrace. The sentence then apposes the agent: Polinestòr ch’ancise Polidoro = 'Polynestor who killed Polydorus.' Note ancise is past tense 'slew'.
segments.purgatorio-20-g116: ultimamente = 'ultimately/at last'. ci = 'to us' (dative clitic). si grida is an impersonal/passive-like construction 'one cries/shouts' or 'it is shouted' — with ci it means 'they shout to us' or 'a shout comes to us.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g117: Crasso is a vocative: the addressee's name (Crassus). The line begins a direct address to this figure.
segments.purgatorio-20-g118: dilci is an archaic/poetic imperative form equivalent to modern dìmmi/dicc(i): 'tell us'. It represents a cliticized/contracted imperative of dire plus the clitic 'ci' (to us).
segments.purgatorio-20-g119: che here introduces a relative/casual clause 'since/you who'. ’l is the clitic object 'it' (el) referring to the matter in question; sai is 2nd-person singular present 'you know'. Together 'che ’l sai' = 'since you know it' or 'you who know it.'
segments.purgatorio-20-g120: di = 'of'. che = interrogative 'what' (introducing 'of what taste'); sapore = 'taste/flavour'. è = present 'is' (copula). l’ oro = 'the gold' (l' = the). The whole is the direct question 'of what taste is the gold?' i.e. 'what does gold taste like?'

sentences.purgatorio-20-s5: We walked with slow, scant steps, and I, attentive to the shadows, heard them piteously weeping and lamenting; and by chance I heard, “Sweet Mary!”
sentences.purgatorio-20-s6: Before us they cried out thus in tears, like a woman who is in childbirth; and they continued: “You were so poor, as can be seen from that lodging where you pledged your holy burden.”
sentences.purgatorio-20-s7: Next I heard: “O good Fabrizio, you chose poverty — indeed virtue — rather than to possess great wealth through vice.”
sentences.purgatorio-20-s8: These words had pleased me so much that I drew myself forward to gain knowledge of that spirit from which they seemed to have come.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s1: Evil will fights against a better will; therefore, against my pleasure, to please him I drew from the water the sponge that does not satisfy.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s2: I moved; and my guide moved himself through the open places along the rock, as one goes along a narrow wall toward the battlements; for the people who pour out, drop by drop through their eyes, the sorrow that fills the whole world, from the other side come out and press too closely.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s3: Accursed be you, ancient she-wolf, who more than all the other beasts have prey because of your hunger without end and gloomy!
sentences.purgatorio-20-s4: O heaven, in whose turning it seems that people believe the conditions here below to be transformed, when will he come by whom this will cease?
sentences.purgatorio-20-s25: So that the future evil and the thing already done may seem less, I see the fiordaliso enter into Alagna, and Christ to be "Catto" in his vicar.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s26: I see him mocked another time; I see the vinegar and the gall renewed, and to be struck down among living thieves.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s27: I see the new Pilate so cruel that this does not satisfy him, but without decree he brings the cupidity-laden sails into the Temple.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s28: O my Lord, when will I be glad to see the vengeance which, hidden, makes your anger sweet in your secret?
sentences.purgatorio-20-s9: He was still speaking about the generosity that Niccolò showed to the maidens, to bring their youth to honor.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s10: "O soul that speaks so well, tell me who you were," I said, "and why you alone renew these worthy praises."
sentences.purgatorio-20-s11: Your word will not be without mercy, if I return to complete the short path of that life which hastens to its end.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s12: And he: 'I will tell you, not for the comfort that I expect anything there, but because so great a grace shines in you before you are dead.'

sentences.purgatorio-20-s22: From this he will win not land, but will gain sin and shame — a fault all the more serious for him the more such a slight injury is reckoned.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s23: The other — who already went out, taken from the ship — I see selling his daughter and bargaining over her, as corsairs do with other slaves.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s24: O avarice, what more can you do with it, since you have drawn my blood to you so, that it no longer cares for its own flesh?
sentences.purgatorio-20-s17: While the great Provençal dowry did not take the shame from my blood, it was of little value, but nevertheless it did no harm.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s18: There his robbery began with force and with deceit; and afterward, as amends, he took Ponthieu and Normandy and Gascony.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s19: Charles came into Italy and, as amends, made Curradino a victim; and then he sent Thomas back to heaven, as amends.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s20: I see a time, not long after this, when another Charles leads forth from France, in order to make both himself and his men better known.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s13: I was the root of the evil plant that darkens the whole Christian land,
sentences.purgatorio-20-s14: But if Douai, Lille, Ghent and Bruges could (act), soon there would be vengeance for it; and I ask it of Him who judges all.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s15: Over there I was called Ugo Ciappetta;
sentences.purgatorio-20-s16: I was the son of a butcher of Paris: when the old kings all fell away, except one who had returned in gray garments, I found the reins of the government of the realm fastened in my hands, and so much power from recent acquisition, and so full of friends, that the head of my son was raised to the widow's crown; from him the sacred bones of these people begin.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s37: Then from every side there began such a cry that the master turned toward me, saying, "Do not doubt while I guide you."
sentences.purgatorio-20-s38: "Gloria in excelsis Deo," they all were saying; from what I understood from those near me, whence I was able to make out the cry.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s39: We were not standing motionless and suspended like the shepherds who first heard that song, until the trembling ceased and I completed it.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s40: Then we resumed our holy walk, looking at the shades that lay on the ground, already returned to their accustomed weeping.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s34: Sometimes one speaks loudly and another softly, according to the inclination that urges us to go — now at a quicker pace and now at a slower one; therefore, with regard to the good that is discussed here by day, I was not the only one before; but here, close by, no other person raised their voice.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s35: We had already departed from it, and were striving to overtake the road as far as power permitted, when I heard — as if something were falling — the mountain tremble; whence a chill seized me such as one is wont to feel who is going to death.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s36: Certainly Delos never shook so violently before Leto made her nest in it to give birth to the two eyes of heaven.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s41: Never did any ignorance, with so great a struggle, make me eager to know; if my memory does not err in this, how much I then seemed to have, thinking; nor, through haste, did I dare to ask, nor could I there see anything for myself: thus I went on timid and thoughtful.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s29: What I was saying about that sole Bride of the Holy Spirit, and how she caused you to turn toward me for some brief rejoinder, is answered to all our prayers as long as the day lasts; but when it grows night, we take a contrary tone on that occasion.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s30: We repeat Pygmalion thereupon; whose craving for gold made him a traitor and a thief and a parricide; and the misery of avaricious Midas, who followed his greedy request, for which one must always laugh.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s31: Then every one remembers the foolish Achan, how he stole the spoils, so that Joshua's wrath here seems still to gnaw at him.
sentences.purgatorio-20-s32: Then we accuse Sapphira together with her husband; we praise the kicks that Heliodorus received; and all the mountain turns in infamy at Polinestor who slew Polydorus; finally someone shouts to us: 'Crassus, tell us — since you know it: what is the taste of gold?'
